text.title: Error Recovery — Nielsen Heuristic #9 Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Error Recovery — Nielsen Heuristic #9 application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every error message MUST contain all three components: what happened + why + how to fix. Messages missing any component are rejected.
rule.rationale: Missing components leave users stuck; "An error occurred" without why or fix is the canonical bad message.

rule.statement: Messages MUST be in plain language; jargon, error codes alone, and stack traces are rejected for end-user surfaces.
rule.rationale: Jargon-laden messages produce support tickets; plain language resolves errors in-product.

rule.statement: The "how to fix" component MUST be actionable: a specific button, link, retry path, or instruction. Vague "try again later" is rejected when a concrete path exists.
rule.rationale: Specific actionable fixes reduce abandonment; vague suggestions push the user to support.

rule.statement: Messages MUST be blame-neutral: no "you did wrong" framing. The system shares responsibility.
rule.rationale: Blame framing degrades trust; neutral framing keeps the user engaged with the fix.

rule.statement: Drafted messages MUST be tested with non-technical users before ship. Untested messages are rejected.
rule.rationale: Designers underestimate jargon; non-technical user testing surfaces the gaps.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
